paragraphs.text: THE INTELLECTUAL ASPECT We will not be able to obtain a definite answer to every question. The greater number of them will remain unanswered.

People think and think, but they cannot come up with anything definite. That is why they say, “Maybe it is this way, maybe it is that way. This yields opinions and suppositions^ in total, don t we have more of these than definite cognitions?

When someone, in discussing a certain class of objects, obtains from himself and from others through borrowing so many definite thoughts and concepts, and manages to add such good opinions and suppositions to the unresolved items, that it is possible to consider this realm of objects sufficiently wellknown and understood; then all that has been obtained is put in order, is set out in the proper relation and sequence, and gives us a science of those objects. Science is the crown of the intellectual labor of the intellect.

I will explain all of this to you later, in order to make clear to you of what the natural and proper operation of our intellectual faculty must consist. It would be proper to discuss diligently what is as yet unknown, in order to come to know it.

Very few people are capable of being scientists, and not everyone is able to study the sciences, but everyone is capable of and must discuss the things surrounding us, in order to obtain definite concepts about them. That is the proper undertaking of the intellectual faculty in everyone. How much it will obtain depends upon its strength; but it should always be occupied with the substantial business of thinking things over, and the discussion of reality.

Meanwhile, what do we see in our mental realm? The continual motion of images and notions without any definite purpose or order. Thoughts rise up one after the other, they form into a line, then they intersect each other, race a few steps forward, then go backward, then run sideways, never stopping for anything. This is not reasoning; it is the wandering and 51

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE scattering of thoughts; therefore, this state is the complete opposite ofwhat our intellectual faculty is supposed to be. That is its illness, which is so inculcated in it and so endemic to everyone, that it would be impossible to find a single human being who is capable of continuously conducting the substantial labor of thinking, who is not subject to this scattering and wandering of various thoughts, which divert him from his occupation and pull him in different directions. Often we get lost in thought. What kind of state is this? Here is a description: A thought goes into the memory archive, and with the help of the imagination, it sorts through all the rubbish which has accumulated there, switching from event to event, and, according to the laws of combining notions, it weaves something which has not been experienced together with something which has, and often weaves the impossible together with what has been experienced, doing this until the person “comes to,” and returns to the surrounding reality. People say, “He is deep in thought.

In reality, he is deep in emptiness, and his intellect is not involved in a substantial discussion of any matter. It is the very same thing as the dreams you have when you are sleeping—idle and empty thought. Observe yourself, and you will see that the greater part of our time is spent on such empty and straying thought. Some days (and are there not more of these), not a single substantial thought enters the mind. I ask you to pay attention to this and spend some time solving this problem: Is it proper for a rational creature to act in this way? In the meantime, I shall turn to another sort of mental reality. 5 ^
